Credit Card Fraud Detection

Final Project Report

Nadezhda Shiroglazova, Prathamesh Nehete, Sheng Hu

Module 7 – MSDS 422

Practical Machine Learning

March 2026

Credit Card Fraud Detection¶

Dataset: Credit Card Fraud Detection (Kaggle, MLG-ULB)

Executive Summary¶

Business Problem: Credit card fraud imposes enormous costs on financial institutions, merchants, and consumers. Global card fraud losses exceeded $32 billion in 2021 and are projected to surpass $43 billion by 2026 (Nilson Report 2022). Each fraudulent transaction costs merchants approximately 2.5 times the original transaction value when accounting for chargebacks, lost goods, and operational overhead (LexisNexis Risk Solutions 2023). Our institution requires an automated, real-time fraud detection system that can identify fraud from anonymized transaction records with minimal impact on legitimate customer activity.

Best Model — XGBoost:

Metric Validation Set Final Test Set
PR-AUC (primary) 0.8072 0.8823
F1-Score 0.8372 0.8539
Recall (fraud caught) 73.5% 77.6% (76/98)
Precision 97.3% 95.0%
False Alarm Rate — 0.007% of legitimate transactions

Core Business Benefit: XGBoost catches 77.6% of all fraud while triggering false alerts on only 0.007% of legitimate transactions — approximately 1 in 14,000 good transactions is incorrectly flagged. This balance enables aggressive fraud interception without creating excessive friction for honest customers.

Key Insight: A model that approves every transaction achieves 99.83% accuracy while catching zero fraud. Our best model intercepts three-quarters of fraud cases with negligible collateral impact. Accuracy is not only misleading here — it is actively dangerous as a KPI.

Management Recommendations (summary):

  • Deploy XGBoost in a real-time scoring pipeline with a business-calibrated decision threshold (~0.988)
  • Route flagged transactions to a tiered human review queue; do not auto-decline
  • Monitor V-feature distributions monthly for concept drift; retrain quarterly
  • Integrate third-party data (device fingerprints, merchant risk, geolocation) to push PR-AUC above 0.92
  • Report fraud detection rate and false alarm rate as operational KPIs — never raw accuracy

Problem Statement / Research Objectives¶

Management / Research Question: Can we reliably identify fraudulent credit card transactions from anonymized transaction data, and what patterns distinguish fraud from legitimate purchases?

Credit card fraud is extraordinarily rare in this dataset — only 0.172% of transactions are fraudulent. This extreme imbalance means that naive detection systems that simply approve every transaction achieve 99.83% accuracy while catching zero fraud. Effective detection therefore requires models and metrics specifically designed for needle-in-a-haystack problems, prioritizing recall (catching fraud) and precision (avoiding false alarms that block legitimate customers) over raw accuracy.

Specific research objectives:

  1. Identify which features most reliably distinguish fraudulent from legitimate transactions, despite PCA anonymization
  2. Evaluate the performance of four ML/DL model families (Logistic Regression, Random Forest, XGBoost, Neural Network) under extreme class imbalance
  3. Determine the appropriate evaluation metrics for this business context and explain why accuracy fails
  4. Produce actionable, quantified business recommendations including deployment strategy, threshold policy, and expected financial impact

Dataset: The dataset was collected by the Machine Learning Group at Université Libre de Bruxelles (ULB) and Worldline, containing 284,807 credit card transactions from European cardholders over two days in September 2013. Due to confidentiality, 28 of the 30 features (V1–V28) have been pre-transformed via Principal Component Analysis (PCA), making them anonymous but orthogonal by construction — a property that fundamentally changes the preprocessing strategy compared to raw financial data (Dal Pozzolo et al. 2015).

Literature Review¶

Credit card fraud detection has attracted sustained academic and industrial attention due to its high economic stakes and inherent machine learning challenges. This review synthesizes recent findings across four themes: the class imbalance problem, tree-based and ensemble methods, deep learning approaches, and sampling strategies. All cited works directly inform the modeling decisions made in this project.

Class Imbalance and Evaluation Methodology¶

The fundamental challenge in fraud detection is extreme class imbalance — fraud typically represents fewer than 0.2% of transactions. Marazqah Btoush et al. (2023), in a systematic review of ML and DL fraud detection methods, document that this imbalance causes standard classifiers to heavily bias toward the majority class, rendering accuracy a misleading metric. Their review consistently identifies Precision-Recall AUC, F1-score, and Matthews Correlation Coefficient (MCC) as the appropriate measures for fraud detection contexts. This finding directly motivates our selection of PR-AUC as the primary evaluation metric in this project.

Karunya et al. (2025) propose a probability-based k-Nearest Neighbors approach specifically designed for imbalanced fraud data, demonstrating that even classical algorithms can be adapted to handle the imbalance problem through probability calibration and distance-weighted voting. Their work reinforces the importance of selecting fraud-specific modifications rather than applying off-the-shelf classifiers without adjustment.

Sampling Strategies for Imbalanced Data¶

Popova and Gardi (2024) systematically evaluate hybrid sampling strategies — combinations of SMOTE oversampling and undersampling — for fraud detection. Their findings indicate hybrid approaches outperform pure SMOTE or random undersampling alone, particularly when combined with ensemble learners. However, they warn that aggressive oversampling on training data can introduce overfitting artifacts when synthesized minority samples do not reflect real fraud pattern diversity. This informed our decision to use class-weight balancing as the primary imbalance correction mechanism.

Fariha et al. (2025) focus on behavioral feature engineering for fraud detection, showing that temporal and behavioral features — transaction velocity, time-of-day patterns, and amount distributions — contribute meaningful signal beyond raw transaction attributes. Their work supports our engineering of cyclical time features (Hour_Sin, Hour_Cos), night-hour indicators, and log-transformed amount features, and suggests that richer behavioral features could further improve performance in future model versions.

Tree-Based and Ensemble Methods¶

Marazqah Btoush et al. (2023) find that gradient-boosting methods — XGBoost in particular — consistently achieve state-of-the-art performance on structured, tabular fraud datasets. Their review attributes this to gradient boosting's ability to model complex non-linear interactions, its native support for imbalanced class weights, and its computational efficiency during inference (sub-millisecond per transaction). Random Forests are identified as the most reliable second-tier performer due to ensemble diversity and robustness to overfitting. These findings align with our empirical results, where XGBoost achieved the highest PR-AUC (0.8823 on the test set) followed closely by Random Forest.

Deep Learning Approaches¶

Mienye and Jere (2024) survey deep learning approaches for fraud detection, noting that neural networks with specialized loss functions — particularly Focal Loss — outperform standard binary cross-entropy when fraud events are rare. Focal Loss down-weights the contribution of easy-to-classify examples (the vast majority of legitimate transactions), forcing the network to focus its learning on harder fraud cases. Our Neural Network implementation adopts this approach (Focal Loss with γ=2, α=0.65), validating the Mienye and Jere finding in the credit fraud context.

Alarfaj et al. (2022) investigate CNN-based architectures for fraud detection, showing that convolutional layers can extract local feature interaction patterns from transaction attribute sequences. Their regularization techniques — batch normalization, dropout, L2 penalties — directly inform our neural network architecture. The decreasing funnel design (256→128→64→32→1) and our use of Batch Normalization and Dropout after each dense layer reflect these architectural recommendations.

Synthesis and Implications for This Project¶

The literature converges on five consistent findings that shaped our methodology:

  1. PR-AUC and F1 are the appropriate evaluation metrics for extreme class imbalance
  2. Gradient-boosted trees are the most reliable performers on tabular fraud data
  3. Neural networks require careful regularization and specialized loss functions to be competitive
  4. Hybrid sampling or class-weight adjustment is preferable to no imbalance correction
  5. Behavioral and temporal feature engineering adds meaningful signal beyond raw attributes

1. Setup and Data Loading¶

Methodology: Tooling, Environment, and Hardware¶

Programming Language and Runtime¶

  • Language: Python 3.11
  • Notebook Environment: Jupyter Notebook (via Anaconda distribution) — interactive, cell-by-cell execution for reproducible analysis

Core Libraries¶

Library Version (approx.) Purpose
NumPy 1.26 Numerical array operations
Pandas 2.1 Data loading, manipulation, summary statistics
Matplotlib / Seaborn 3.8 / 0.13 EDA visualization and results plotting
Scikit-learn 1.4 Preprocessing (RobustScaler, stratified splits), Logistic Regression, Random Forest, RandomizedSearchCV, GridSearchCV, all evaluation metrics
XGBoost 2.0 Gradient-boosted tree classifier; native scale_pos_weight and aucpr eval metric
TensorFlow / Keras 2.15 Neural network construction, Focal Loss, Early Stopping, ReduceLROnPlateau callbacks
imbalanced-learn 0.12 SMOTE and hybrid sampling utilities (evaluated; class-weight approach selected for final models)
kagglehub latest Dataset download from Kaggle

Hardware and Compute Environment¶

  • Primary Environment: Local CPU-based machine (standard laptop, no GPU acceleration)
  • Neural Network Training: TensorFlow CPU execution. With early stopping (patience=25), training converged in approximately 40–80 epochs within a practical timeframe on CPU
  • Tree Models: Scikit-learn and XGBoost use multi-threaded CPU parallelism (n_jobs=-1). Hyperparameter search runs (100 RandomizedSearch × 5-fold CV) completed in 10–30 minutes per model
  • Dataset Storage: creditcard.csv (~143 MB) loaded entirely into memory via Pandas; no external database or cloud storage required
  • Reproducibility: All random seeds fixed at random_state=42 across splits, model initialization, and search procedures

Model Selection and Evaluation Approach¶

We implement and evaluate four ML/DL model families as required:

# Model Type Imbalance Handling
1 Logistic Regression Linear baseline class_weight='balanced'
2 Random Forest Ensemble (bagging) class_weight='balanced'
3 XGBoost Ensemble (boosting) scale_pos_weight ≈ 578
4 Neural Network (BCE + Focal Loss) Deep Learning class_weight + Focal Loss

Primary evaluation metric: PR-AUC (Average Precision) — the 0.172% fraud rate makes accuracy meaningless and ROC-AUC misleadingly optimistic. Secondary metrics: F1-Score, Recall, Precision, MCC.

Hyperparameter tuning: RandomizedSearchCV (RF, XGBoost: 20 iterations × 5-fold StratifiedKFold) and GridSearchCV (Logistic Regression: exhaustive 24-combination grid × 5-fold).

path = kagglehub.dataset_download("mlg-ulb/creditcardfraud")

files = os.listdir(path)
csv_file = [f for f in files if f.endswith('.csv')][0]
df = pd.read_csv(os.path.join(path, csv_file))

2. Initial Exploratory Data Analysis¶

Dataset Context¶

Features V1–V28 are principal components obtained via PCA from the original (confidential) transaction features. This has critical implications:

  1. V1–V28 are orthogonal by construction — there should be near-zero correlation between them. If we find high correlations, something is wrong.
  2. V1–V28 are already centered and scaled — they do not need StandardScaler.
  3. Feature names are anonymous — we cannot do domain-driven feature engineering on V-features. Our engineering is limited to Time and Amount.
  4. No multicollinearity removal is needed — PCA components are independent.

The only raw (non-PCA) features are:

  • Time - seconds elapsed since the first transaction in the dataset
  • Amount - the transaction amount in euros

desc['range'] = desc['max'] - desc['min']
desc['iqr'] = df.apply(lambda x: iqr(x))

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(18, 5))

counts = df['Class'].value_counts()
colors = ['#2ecc71', '#e74c3c']
bars = axes[0].bar(['Legitimate', 'Fraud'], counts.values, color=colors)
axes[0].set_ylabel('Count')
axes[0].set_title('Transaction Counts')
for bar, val in zip(bars, counts.values):
    axes[0].text(bar.get_x() + bar.get_width() / 2, val + 2000,
                 f'{val:,}', ha='center', fontweight='bold', fontsize=11)

axes[1].bar(['Legitimate', 'Fraud'], counts.values, color=colors)
axes[1].set_yscale('log')
axes[1].set_ylabel('Count (log scale)')
axes[1].set_title('Transaction Counts (Log Scale)')
for i, val in enumerate(counts.values):
    axes[1].text(i, val * 1.3, f'{val:,}', ha='center', fontweight='bold')

axes[2].pie(counts.values, labels=['Legitimate\n(99.83%)', 'Fraud\n(0.17%)'],
            colors=colors, startangle=90, explode=(0, 0.15),
            autopct='%1.3f%%', textprops={'fontsize': 11})
axes[2].set_title('Class Distribution')

plt.suptitle('Extreme Class Imbalance: Only 0.172% Fraud',
             fontsize=15, fontweight='bold')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(16, 5))

axes[0].hist(df[df['Class'] == 0]['Time'], bins=100, alpha=0.6,
             color='#2ecc71', label='Legitimate', density=True)
axes[0].hist(df[df['Class'] == 1]['Time'], bins=100, alpha=0.7,
             color='#e74c3c', label='Fraud', density=True)
axes[0].set_xlabel('Time (seconds)')
axes[0].set_ylabel('Density')
axes[0].set_title('Transaction Time Distribution by Class')
axes[0].legend()

df['Hour'] = (df['Time'] / 3600) % 24
axes[1].hist(df[df['Class'] == 0]['Hour'], bins=48, alpha=0.6,
             color='#2ecc71', label='Legitimate', density=True)
axes[1].hist(df[df['Class'] == 1]['Hour'], bins=48, alpha=0.7,
             color='#e74c3c', label='Fraud', density=True)
axes[1].set_xlabel('Hour of Day')
axes[1].set_ylabel('Density')
axes[1].set_title('Hour-of-Day Distribution by Class')
axes[1].legend()

plt.suptitle('Time Feature Analysis', fontsize=14, fontweight='bold')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

df.drop('Hour', axis=1, inplace=True)

fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 2, figsize=(16, 10))

axes[0, 0].hist(df[df['Class'] == 0]['Amount'], bins=100, alpha=0.6,
                color='#2ecc71', label='Legitimate', density=True)
axes[0, 0].hist(df[df['Class'] == 1]['Amount'], bins=100, alpha=0.7,
                color='#e74c3c', label='Fraud', density=True)
axes[0, 0].set_xlabel('Amount (€)')
axes[0, 0].set_title('Amount Distribution (Raw)')
axes[0, 0].legend()

axes[0, 1].hist(np.log1p(df[df['Class'] == 0]['Amount']), bins=80, alpha=0.6,
                color='#2ecc71', label='Legitimate', density=True)
axes[0, 1].hist(np.log1p(df[df['Class'] == 1]['Amount']), bins=80, alpha=0.7,
                color='#e74c3c', label='Fraud', density=True)
axes[0, 1].set_xlabel('log(1 + Amount)')
axes[0, 1].set_title('Amount Distribution (Log Transformed)')
axes[0, 1].legend()

sns.boxplot(x='Class', y='Amount', data=df, ax=axes[1, 0],
            hue='Class', palette={0: '#2ecc71', 1: '#e74c3c'},
            showfliers=False, legend=False)
axes[1, 0].set_xticklabels(['Legitimate', 'Fraud'])
axes[1, 0].set_title('Amount by Class (Outliers Hidden)')

amount_stats = df.groupby('Class')['Amount'].describe()
axes[1, 1].axis('off')
table = axes[1, 1].table(
    cellText=amount_stats[['mean', '50%', 'std', 'min', 'max']].round(2).values,
    rowLabels=['Legitimate', 'Fraud'],
    colLabels=['Mean', 'Median', 'Std', 'Min', 'Max'],
    loc='center', cellLoc='center'
)
table.auto_set_font_size(False)
table.set_fontsize(11)
table.scale(1.2, 1.8)
axes[1, 1].set_title('Amount Statistics by Class', fontsize=12, pad=20)

plt.suptitle('Amount Feature Analysis', fontsize=14, fontweight='bold')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

print(f"\nAmount statistics:")
print(f"  Legitimate — Mean: €{df[df['Class']==0]['Amount'].mean():.2f}, "
      f"Median: €{df[df['Class']==0]['Amount'].median():.2f}")
print(f"  Fraud      — Mean: €{df[df['Class']==1]['Amount'].mean():.2f}, "
      f"Median: €{df[df['Class']==1]['Amount'].median():.2f}")

Amount statistics:
  Legitimate — Mean: €88.29, Median: €22.00
  Fraud      — Mean: €122.21, Median: €9.25
In [10]:
skew_kurt = pd.DataFrame({
    'Skewness': df.drop('Class', axis=1).apply(skew),
    'Kurtosis': df.drop('Class', axis=1).apply(kurtosis),
    '|Skewness|': df.drop('Class', axis=1).apply(lambda x: abs(skew(x))),
}).sort_values('|Skewness|', ascending=False)

print(skew_kurt.head(20).to_string())

print(f"\nFeatures with |skewness| > 2:  {(skew_kurt['|Skewness|'] > 2).sum()}")
print(f"Features with kurtosis > 10:   {(skew_kurt['Kurtosis'] > 10).sum()}")
print(f"Features with kurtosis > 100:  {(skew_kurt['Kurtosis'] > 100).sum()}")

        Skewness  Kurtosis  |Skewness|
Amount   16.9776  845.0778     16.9776
V28      11.1920  933.3811     11.1920

In [11]:
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(16, 6))

kurt_sorted = skew_kurt.sort_values('Kurtosis', ascending=False)
colors = ['#e74c3c' if k > 10 else '#3498db' for k in kurt_sorted['Kurtosis']]
axes[0].barh(kurt_sorted.index, kurt_sorted['Kurtosis'], color=colors)
axes[0].set_xlabel('Kurtosis')
axes[0].set_title('Kurtosis by Feature (Red = Heavy Tails, >10)')
axes[0].axvline(x=10, color='gray', linestyle='--', alpha=0.7)
axes[0].invert_yaxis()

skew_sorted = skew_kurt.sort_values('Skewness', ascending=False)
colors = ['#e74c3c' if abs(s) > 2 else '#3498db' for s in skew_sorted['Skewness']]
axes[1].barh(skew_sorted.index, skew_sorted['Skewness'], color=colors)
axes[1].set_xlabel('Skewness')
axes[1].set_title('Skewness by Feature (Red = |Skew| > 2)')
axes[1].axvline(x=0, color='gray', linestyle='-', alpha=0.5)
axes[1].invert_yaxis()

plt.suptitle('Distribution Shape Analysis', fontsize=14, fontweight='bold')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

3. Expanded EDA — V-Features and Correlations¶

Since V1–V28 are PCA components, they are anonymous. However, we can still analyze which ones separate the fraud and legitimate classes, which ones have outliers, and whether the expected orthogonality holds.

In [12]:
v_features = [f'V{i}' for i in range(1, 29)]

mean_diff = pd.DataFrame({
    'Legit_Mean': df[df['Class'] == 0][v_features].mean(),
    'Fraud_Mean': df[df['Class'] == 1][v_features].mean(),
    'Pooled_Std': df[v_features].std()
})
mean_diff['Standardized_Diff'] = (
    abs(mean_diff['Fraud_Mean'] - mean_diff['Legit_Mean']) / mean_diff['Pooled_Std']
)
mean_diff = mean_diff.sort_values('Standardized_Diff', ascending=False)

print(" V-Features Ranked by Class Separation \n")
print(mean_diff[['Standardized_Diff', 'Legit_Mean', 'Fraud_Mean']].to_string())

 V-Features Ranked by Class Separation 

     Standardized_Diff  Legit_Mean  Fraud_Mean

3.2 VISUALIZATION: Top 8 V-Features by Class Separation¶

In [13]:
top8 = mean_diff.head(8).index.tolist()

fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 4, figsize=(20, 10))

for ax, feat in zip(axes.flatten(), top8):
    ax.hist(df[df['Class'] == 0][feat], bins=80, alpha=0.5,
            color='#2ecc71', label='Legit', density=True)
    ax.hist(df[df['Class'] == 1][feat], bins=80, alpha=0.6,
            color='#e74c3c', label='Fraud', density=True)
    diff = mean_diff.loc[feat, 'Standardized_Diff']
    ax.set_title(f'{feat} (sep={diff:.2f})', fontsize=11)
    ax.legend(fontsize=8)

plt.suptitle('Top 8 V-Features by Class Separation',
             fontsize=14, fontweight='bold')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

3.3 VISUALIZATION: Bottom 4 V-Features (Least Useful)¶

In [14]:
bottom4 = mean_diff.tail(4).index.tolist()

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 4, figsize=(20, 4))

for ax, feat in zip(axes.flatten(), bottom4):
    ax.hist(df[df['Class'] == 0][feat], bins=80, alpha=0.5,
            color='#2ecc71', label='Legit', density=True)
    ax.hist(df[df['Class'] == 1][feat], bins=80, alpha=0.6,
            color='#e74c3c', label='Fraud', density=True)
    diff = mean_diff.loc[feat, 'Standardized_Diff']
    ax.set_title(f'{feat} (sep={diff:.2f})', fontsize=11)
    ax.legend(fontsize=8)

plt.suptitle('Bottom 4 V-Features — Near-Zero Class Separation',
             fontsize=14, fontweight='bold')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Correlation matrix¶

In [15]:
corr_matrix = df.drop('Class', axis=1).corr()

plt.figure(figsize=(16, 13))
mask = np.triu(np.ones_like(corr_matrix, dtype=bool))
sns.heatmap(corr_matrix, mask=mask, cmap='RdBu_r', center=0,
            vmin=-1, vmax=1, linewidths=0.1, square=True,
            xticklabels=True, yticklabels=True)
plt.title('Correlation Matrix — All Features\n'
          '(V-features should be near-zero; only Time/Amount may correlate)',
          fontsize=14, fontweight='bold')
plt.xticks(fontsize=7, rotation=90)
plt.yticks(fontsize=7)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

# Check for unexpected correlations among V-features
v_corr = df[v_features].corr()
upper_v = v_corr.where(np.triu(np.ones(v_corr.shape), k=1).astype(bool))
high_corr_v = [(col, upper_v.index[i], upper_v.iloc[i][col])
               for col in upper_v.columns
               for i in range(len(upper_v))
               if abs(upper_v.iloc[i][col]) > 0.3]

print(f"\nV-feature pairs with |correlation| > 0.3: {len(high_corr_v)}")
if high_corr_v:
    for f1, f2, r in sorted(high_corr_v, key=lambda x: abs(x[2]), reverse=True)[:5]:
        print(f"  {f1} ↔ {f2}: {r:.4f}")
else:
    print("None")

V-feature pairs with |correlation| > 0.3: 0
None

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 8))
colors = ['#e74c3c' if c > 0.1 else '#3498db' for c in target_corr.values]
target_corr.plot(kind='barh', ax=ax, color=colors)
ax.set_xlabel('|Correlation with Class|')

             fontsize=13, fontweight='bold')

ax.legend()
ax.invert_yaxis()

plt.show()

Outlier analysis¶

In [17]:
outlier_pct = pd.Series(dtype=float)
for col in df.drop('Class', axis=1).columns:
    q1 = df[col].quantile(0.25)
    q3 = df[col].quantile(0.75)
    iqr_val = q3 - q1
    lower = q1 - 3 * iqr_val
    upper = q3 + 3 * iqr_val
    n_outliers = ((df[col] < lower) | (df[col] > upper)).sum()
    outlier_pct[col] = n_outliers / len(df) * 100

outlier_pct = outlier_pct.sort_values(ascending=False)

print("Outlier Percentage by Feature (IQR × 3)\n")
print(outlier_pct.head(15).to_string())

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 8))
outlier_pct.plot(kind='barh', ax=ax, color='steelblue')
ax.set_xlabel('Outlier Percentage (%)')
ax.set_title('Outlier Prevalence by Feature (IQR × 3 Method)',
             fontsize=13, fontweight='bold')
ax.invert_yaxis()
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Outlier Percentage by Feature (IQR × 3)

Amount   6.6505
V27      5.8018
V20      4.6997
V28      4.4146
V8       3.9623
V23      2.8563
V21      2.4346
V6       1.8922
V2       1.5635
V10      1.1264
V7       1.0713
V14      0.8444
V5       0.8149
V1       0.7001
V17      0.5088
In [ ]:
# 3.7  Duplicate Analysis
n_dupes = df.duplicated().sum()
print(f"Duplicate rows: {n_dupes}")

if n_dupes > 0:
    # Check if duplicates are more likely to be fraud
    dupes = df[df.duplicated(keep=False)]
    dupe_fraud_rate = dupes['Class'].mean() * 100
    overall_fraud_rate = df['Class'].mean() * 100

    print(f"\nFraud rate in duplicates:     {dupe_fraud_rate:.2f}%")
    print(f"Fraud rate overall:           {overall_fraud_rate:.4f}%")
    print(f"\nDuplicate rows likely represent repeated legitimate transactions")
    print(f"(e.g., same amount at same merchant). We keep them as they are")
    print(f"genuine data points, not data errors.")
else:
    print("No duplicate rows found.")

Duplicate rows: 1081

Fraud rate in duplicates:     1.73%
Fraud rate overall:           0.1727%

Duplicate rows likely represent repeated legitimate transactions
(e.g., same amount at same merchant). We keep them as they are
genuine data points, not data errors.

Hypotheses about Fraud Drivers¶

  1. Certain V-features (especially those with high class separation) will be the primary fraud indicators. The PCA transformation captured latent patterns; the top-separating components likely encode transaction velocity, geographic anomalies, or merchant category signals.

  2. Transaction Amount alone is a weak predictor. Fraudulent transactions span a wide range of amounts. However, extremely small "test charges" (e.g., < €1) and unusually large transactions may be more suspicious.

  3. Time-of-day patterns will provide modest additional signal. Fraud may concentrate during off-peak hours when human monitoring is reduced.

Data Risks Identified¶

  1. Extreme class imbalance (0.172%): Even worse than the bankruptcy dataset (3.2%). Accuracy is completely meaningless here; we must use PR-AUC, recall, and F1 for the minority class.

  2. PCA anonymization limits feature engineering: We cannot create domain-driven composite features from V1–V28 because we don't know what they represent. Engineering is limited to Time and Amount.

  3. Temporal structure: Transactions are time-ordered. A naive random split might leak future information. However, since PCA has already obscured the temporal features, and we are using stratify on the target, a random stratified split is acceptable for this dataset.

4. Data Preparation and Feature Engineering¶

4.1 Train / Validation / Test Split (70 / 10 / 20)¶

We split before any preprocessing to prevent data leakage. Stratification ensures the fraud rate is preserved across all partitions.

In [19]:
X = df.drop('Class', axis=1)
y = df['Class']

X_temp, X_test, y_temp, y_test = train_test_split(
    X, y, test_size=0.20, random_state=SEED, stratify=y
)

X_train, X_val, y_train, y_val = train_test_split(
    X_temp, y_temp, test_size=0.125, random_state=SEED, stratify=y_temp
)

for name, X_part, y_part in [('Training', X_train, y_train),
                              ('Validation', X_val, y_val),
                              ('Test', X_test, y_test)]:
    n = len(y_part)
    n_fraud = y_part.sum()
    print(f"  {name:12s}: {n:>7,} rows ({n/len(df)*100:5.1f}%)  |  "
          f"Fraud: {n_fraud:>4} ({y_part.mean()*100:.4f}%)")

print(f"\n  Total:       {len(y_train)+len(y_val)+len(y_test):>7,}")

  Training    : 199,364 rows ( 70.0%)  |  Fraud:  345 (0.1731%)
  Validation  :  28,481 rows ( 10.0%)  |  Fraud:   49 (0.1720%)
  Test        :  56,962 rows ( 20.0%)  |  Fraud:   98 (0.1720%)

  Total:       284,807

4.2 Feature Engineering¶

Since V1–V28 are anonymous PCA components, our feature engineering is focused on the two interpretable features: Time and Amount.

In [20]:
def engineer_features(data):
    data = data.copy()

    # 1. Hour of day (cyclical)
    # Time is seconds since first transaction; dataset spans ~2 days
    data['Hour'] = (data['Time'] / 3600) % 24

    # 2. Sin/Cos encoding of hour (captures cyclical nature)
    # WHY: Hour 23 is close to hour 0, but raw values are far apart.
    # Sin/cos encoding preserves this cyclical proximity.
    data['Hour_Sin'] = np.sin(2 * np.pi * data['Hour'] / 24)
    data['Hour_Cos'] = np.cos(2 * np.pi * data['Hour'] / 24)

    # 3. Is nighttime (off-peak hours: 11 PM - 6 AM)
    # WHY: Fraud may concentrate during off-peak hours.
    data['Is_Night'] = ((data['Hour'] >= 23) | (data['Hour'] < 6)).astype(int)

    # 4. Log-transformed Amount
    # WHY: Amount is extremely right-skewed (skewness ~16).
    # Log transform reduces skew and makes the distribution more normal,
    # which helps distance-based and split-based algorithms.
    data['Log_Amount'] = np.log1p(data['Amount'])

    # 5. Amount bins (small, medium, large, very large)
    # WHY: Captures non-linear relationship between amount and fraud risk.
    # Very small amounts may be "test charges" by fraudsters.
    # Bins are based on fixed thresholds, not data-dependent quantiles.
    data['Amount_Bin_Small'] = (data['Amount'] <= 5).astype(int)
    data['Amount_Bin_Large'] = (data['Amount'] > 500).astype(int)

    # Drop raw Hour (keep sin/cos representation)
    data.drop('Hour', axis=1, inplace=True)

    return data


X_train_eng = engineer_features(X_train)
X_val_eng = engineer_features(X_val)
X_test_eng = engineer_features(X_test)

new_cols = [c for c in X_train_eng.columns if c not in X_train.columns]
print(f"Engineered features: {new_cols}")
print(f"\nFeature count: {X_train.shape[1]} → {X_train_eng.shape[1]}")

print(f"\nEngineered feature statistics (training set):")
for col in new_cols:
    corr = np.corrcoef(X_train_eng[col], y_train)[0, 1]
    print(f"  {col:20s}  mean={X_train_eng[col].mean():.4f}  "
          f"std={X_train_eng[col].std():.4f}  corr_with_fraud={corr:.4f}")

Engineered features: ['Hour_Sin', 'Hour_Cos', 'Is_Night', 'Log_Amount', 'Amount_Bin_Small', 'Amount_Bin_Large']

Feature count: 30 → 36

Engineered feature statistics (training set):
  Hour_Sin              mean=-0.2673  std=0.6274  corr_with_fraud=0.0116
  Hour_Cos              mean=-0.1416  std=0.7175  corr_with_fraud=0.0056
  Is_Night              mean=0.1219  std=0.3272  corr_with_fraud=0.0206
  Log_Amount            mean=3.1527  std=1.6563  corr_with_fraud=-0.0087
  Amount_Bin_Small      mean=0.2424  std=0.4286  corr_with_fraud=0.0201
  Amount_Bin_Large      mean=0.0319  std=0.1757  corr_with_fraud=0.0096
In [21]:
fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 3, figsize=(18, 10))

for label, color, name in [(0, '#2ecc71', 'Legit'), (1, '#e74c3c', 'Fraud')]:
    subset = X_train_eng.loc[y_train == label]
    axes[0, 0].hist(subset['Hour_Sin'], bins=50, alpha=0.5, color=color,
                    label=name, density=True)
axes[0, 0].set_title('Hour_Sin by Class')
axes[0, 0].legend()

for label, color, name in [(0, '#2ecc71', 'Legit'), (1, '#e74c3c', 'Fraud')]:
    subset = X_train_eng.loc[y_train == label]
    axes[0, 1].hist(subset['Hour_Cos'], bins=50, alpha=0.5, color=color,
                    label=name, density=True)
axes[0, 1].set_title('Hour_Cos by Class')
axes[0, 1].legend()

night_data = pd.DataFrame({
    'Is_Night': X_train_eng['Is_Night'],
    'Class': y_train.values
})
night_fraud = night_data.groupby('Is_Night')['Class'].mean() * 100
axes[0, 2].bar(['Daytime', 'Nighttime'], night_fraud.values,
               color=['#3498db', '#e74c3c'])
axes[0, 2].set_ylabel('Fraud Rate (%)')
axes[0, 2].set_title('Fraud Rate: Day vs Night')
for i, v in enumerate(night_fraud.values):
    axes[0, 2].text(i, v + 0.01, f'{v:.3f}%', ha='center', fontweight='bold')

for label, color, name in [(0, '#2ecc71', 'Legit'), (1, '#e74c3c', 'Fraud')]:
    subset = X_train_eng.loc[y_train == label]
    axes[1, 0].hist(subset['Log_Amount'], bins=60, alpha=0.5, color=color,
                    label=name, density=True)
axes[1, 0].set_title('Log_Amount by Class')
axes[1, 0].legend()

small_data = pd.DataFrame({
    'Small': X_train_eng['Amount_Bin_Small'],
    'Class': y_train.values
})
small_fraud = small_data.groupby('Small')['Class'].mean() * 100
axes[1, 1].bar(['Amount > €5', 'Amount ≤ €5'], small_fraud.values,
               color=['#3498db', '#e74c3c'])
axes[1, 1].set_ylabel('Fraud Rate (%)')
axes[1, 1].set_title('Fraud Rate: Small vs Normal Amounts')
for i, v in enumerate(small_fraud.values):
    axes[1, 1].text(i, v + 0.01, f'{v:.3f}%', ha='center', fontweight='bold')

large_data = pd.DataFrame({
    'Large': X_train_eng['Amount_Bin_Large'],
    'Class': y_train.values
})
large_fraud = large_data.groupby('Large')['Class'].mean() * 100
axes[1, 2].bar(['Amount ≤ €500', 'Amount > €500'], large_fraud.values,
               color=['#3498db', '#e74c3c'])
axes[1, 2].set_ylabel('Fraud Rate (%)')
axes[1, 2].set_title('Fraud Rate: Large vs Normal Amounts')
for i, v in enumerate(large_fraud.values):
    axes[1, 2].text(i, v + 0.01, f'{v:.3f}%', ha='center', fontweight='bold')

plt.suptitle('Engineered Feature Analysis (Training Set Only)',
             fontsize=14, fontweight='bold')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

4.3 Outlier Treatment: Winsorization of Amount¶

The V-features are PCA components and their outliers are meaningful (they encode extreme transaction patterns). We only winsorize Amount because it has extreme right skew (skewness 16) and the max (€25,691) is orders of magnitude above the median (€22).

Winsorization caps are computed on training data only.

In [22]:
amount_lower = X_train_eng['Amount'].quantile(0.001)
amount_upper = X_train_eng['Amount'].quantile(0.999)

print(f"Amount winsorization caps (from training):")
print(f"  Lower (0.1th pct): €{amount_lower:.2f}")
print(f"  Upper (99.9th pct): €{amount_upper:.2f}")
print(f"  Training Amount range before: €{X_train_eng['Amount'].min():.2f} — "
      f"€{X_train_eng['Amount'].max():.2f}")

for data in [X_train_eng, X_val_eng, X_test_eng]:
    data['Amount'] = data['Amount'].clip(lower=amount_lower, upper=amount_upper)

print(f"  Training Amount range after:  €{X_train_eng['Amount'].min():.2f} — "
      f"€{X_train_eng['Amount'].max():.2f}")

print(f"\nAmount skewness before winsorization: "
      f"{skew(X_train['Amount']):.2f}")
print(f"Amount skewness after winsorization:  "
      f"{skew(X_train_eng['Amount']):.2f}")
print(f"Log_Amount skewness:                  "
      f"{skew(X_train_eng['Log_Amount']):.2f}")

Amount winsorization caps (from training):
  Lower (0.1th pct): €0.00
  Upper (99.9th pct): €2929.93
  Training Amount range before: €0.00 — €19656.53
  Training Amount range after:  €0.00 — €2929.93

Amount skewness before winsorization: 14.83
Amount skewness after winsorization:  6.81
Log_Amount skewness:                  0.16

4.4 Scaling Strategy¶

  • V1–V28: Already standardized by PCA. No additional scaling needed.
  • Amount: Needs scaling — it's on a completely different scale (€0–€25k) compared to V-features (centered at 0, std ≈ 1–5).
  • Time: Raw seconds (0–172,000). Needs scaling.
  • Engineered features: Log_Amount is on a moderate scale; Hour_Sin/Cos are bounded [-1, 1]; Is_Night and Amount_Bins are binary (0/1).

We use RobustScaler for Amount and Time because it is less sensitive to outliers than StandardScaler. Scaler is fit on training data only.

In [23]:
features_to_scale = ['Amount', 'Time', 'Log_Amount']

scaler = RobustScaler()
X_train_eng[features_to_scale] = scaler.fit_transform(X_train_eng[features_to_scale])
X_val_eng[features_to_scale] = scaler.transform(X_val_eng[features_to_scale])
X_test_eng[features_to_scale] = scaler.transform(X_test_eng[features_to_scale])

print(f"\nScaled features: {features_to_scale}")
print(f"\nTraining statistics after scaling:")
for col in features_to_scale:
    print(f"  {col:15s}: mean={X_train_eng[col].mean():.4f}, "
          f"std={X_train_eng[col].std():.4f}, "
          f"median={X_train_eng[col].median():.4f}")

print(f"\nV-features (not scaled) — sample statistics:")
for col in ['V1', 'V14', 'V17']:
    print(f"  {col:15s}: mean={X_train_eng[col].mean():.4f}, "
          f"std={X_train_eng[col].std():.4f}")

Scaled features: ['Amount', 'Time', 'Log_Amount']

Training statistics after scaling:
  Amount         : mean=0.8980, std=2.9545, median=0.0000
  Time           : mean=0.1177, std=0.5576, median=0.0000
  Log_Amount     : mean=0.0070, std=0.6685, median=0.0000

V-features (not scaled) — sample statistics:
  V1             : mean=0.0004, std=1.9602
  V14            : mean=-0.0005, std=0.9574
  V17            : mean=-0.0000, std=0.8472

4.5 Near-Zero Variance Check¶

In [24]:
variance = X_train_eng.var().sort_values()
low_var = variance[variance < 1e-6]

print(f"Features with near-zero variance: {len(low_var)}")
if len(low_var) > 0:
    print(low_var.to_string())
    # Drop them
    drop_cols = low_var.index.tolist()
    X_train_eng.drop(columns=drop_cols, inplace=True)
    X_val_eng.drop(columns=drop_cols, inplace=True)
    X_test_eng.drop(columns=drop_cols, inplace=True)
    print(f"\nDropped: {drop_cols}")
else:
    print("All features have sufficient variance. ")

print(f"\nFinal feature count: {X_train_eng.shape[1]}")

Features with near-zero variance: 0
All features have sufficient variance. 

Final feature count: 36

4.6 Final Dataset Overview and Validation¶

In [25]:
print(f"\n  Training:   {X_train_eng.shape}")
print(f"  Validation: {X_val_eng.shape}")
print(f"  Test:       {X_test_eng.shape}")

print(f"\n  Missing values (train): {X_train_eng.isnull().sum().sum()}")
print(f"  Missing values (val):   {X_val_eng.isnull().sum().sum()}")
print(f"  Missing values (test):  {X_test_eng.isnull().sum().sum()}")

print(f"\n  Infinite values (train): {np.isinf(X_train_eng).sum().sum()}")

for data in [X_train_eng, X_val_eng, X_test_eng]:
    data.replace([np.inf, -np.inf], np.nan, inplace=True)
    data.fillna(0, inplace=True)

print(f"  After cleanup — Missing: {X_train_eng.isnull().sum().sum()}, "
      f"Inf: {np.isinf(X_train_eng).sum().sum()}")

print(f"\n  All dtypes numeric: "
      f"{all(X_train_eng.dtypes.apply(lambda x: np.issubdtype(x, np.number)))}")

print(f"\n  Features: {X_train_eng.columns.tolist()}")

  Training:   (199364, 36)
  Validation: (28481, 36)
  Test:       (56962, 36)

  Missing values (test):  0

In [26]:
feature_summary = pd.DataFrame({
    'Feature': X_train_eng.columns,
    'Mean': X_train_eng.mean().values,
    'Std': X_train_eng.std().values,
    'Min': X_train_eng.min().values,
    'Max': X_train_eng.max().values,
    'Skewness': X_train_eng.apply(skew).values,
    'Kurtosis': X_train_eng.apply(kurtosis).values,
    'Corr_with_Fraud': [np.corrcoef(X_train_eng[c], y_train)[0, 1]
                         for c in X_train_eng.columns]
}).set_index('Feature')

feature_summary['|Corr|'] = feature_summary['Corr_with_Fraud'].abs()
feature_summary = feature_summary.sort_values('|Corr|', ascending=False)

print(feature_summary.to_string())

5. Comparison: Original vs Prepared Data¶

In [27]:
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(18, 5))

axes[0].hist(X_train['Amount'], bins=80, color='steelblue', alpha=0.7, density=True)
axes[0].set_title(f'ORIGINAL: Amount\n(skew={skew(X_train["Amount"]):.2f},'
                  f' kurt={kurtosis(X_train["Amount"]):.1f})')
axes[0].set_xlabel('Amount (€)')

axes[1].hist(X_train_eng['Amount'], bins=80, color='darkorange', alpha=0.7, density=True)
axes[1].set_title(f'SCALED: Amount (RobustScaler)\n'
                  f'(skew={skew(X_train_eng["Amount"]):.2f})')
axes[1].set_xlabel('Scaled Amount')

axes[2].hist(X_train_eng['Log_Amount'], bins=80, color='#9b59b6', alpha=0.7, density=True)
axes[2].set_title(f'ENGINEERED: Log_Amount\n'
                  f'(skew={skew(X_train_eng["Log_Amount"]):.2f})')
axes[2].set_xlabel('Scaled log(1 + Amount)')

plt.suptitle('Amount Transformation: Original → Scaled → Log-Transformed',
             fontsize=14, fontweight='bold')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
In [28]:
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(18, 5))

axes[0].hist(X_train['Time'], bins=80, color='steelblue', alpha=0.7, density=True)
axes[0].set_title('ORIGINAL: Time (seconds)')

axes[1].hist(X_train_eng['Time'], bins=80, color='darkorange', alpha=0.7, density=True)
axes[1].set_title('SCALED: Time (RobustScaler)')

axes[2].scatter(X_train_eng['Hour_Sin'], X_train_eng['Hour_Cos'],
                alpha=0.01, s=1, color='steelblue')
fraud_mask = y_train == 1
axes[2].scatter(X_train_eng.loc[fraud_mask, 'Hour_Sin'],
                X_train_eng.loc[fraud_mask, 'Hour_Cos'],
                alpha=0.5, s=10, color='red', label='Fraud')
axes[2].set_xlabel('Hour_Sin')
axes[2].set_ylabel('Hour_Cos')
axes[2].set_title('Cyclical Hour Encoding')
axes[2].legend()

plt.suptitle('Time Transformation: Original → Scaled → Cyclical Encoding',
             fontsize=14, fontweight='bold')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
In [29]:
eng_features = ['Amount', 'Time', 'Log_Amount', 'Hour_Sin', 'Hour_Cos',
                'Is_Night', 'Amount_Bin_Small', 'Amount_Bin_Large']
eng_features = [f for f in eng_features if f in X_train_eng.columns]

top_v = ['V14', 'V17', 'V12', 'V10', 'V3']
plot_cols = eng_features + top_v

corr_final = X_train_eng[plot_cols].copy()
corr_final['Fraud'] = y_train.values
corr_matrix_final = corr_final.corr()

plt.figure(figsize=(12, 10))
mask = np.triu(np.ones_like(corr_matrix_final, dtype=bool))
sns.heatmap(corr_matrix_final, mask=mask, annot=True, fmt='.2f',
            cmap='RdBu_r', center=0, vmin=-1, vmax=1,
            linewidths=0.5, square=True)
plt.title('Correlation Matrix — Engineered Features + Top V-Features + Target',
          fontsize=13, fontweight='bold')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

6. Data Leakage Prevention Checklist¶

Check Status
Data split performed before any preprocessing done
Winsorization caps computed on training data only done
RobustScaler fit on training data only, then .transform() on val/test done
Feature engineering uses only element-wise operations(no fitting) done
Bin thresholds are fixed constants (€5, €500), not data-dependent done
Target variable Class is never used as a feature or in any transformation done
Fraud rates are consistent across train/val/test (~0.172%) done
No future transactions leak into past (stratified random split) done
V1–V28 PCA features are not re-scaled (already standardized) done

7. Data Ready for Modeling¶

The following objects are prepared and ready for model training:

Object Shape Description
X_train_eng (train_rows, features) Training features, preprocessed
y_train (train_rows,) Training labels
X_val_eng (val_rows, features) Validation features, preprocessed
y_val (val_rows,) Validation labels
X_test_eng (test_rows, features) Test features, preprocessed (untouched until final eval)
y_test (test_rows,) Test labels

Preprocessing Summary¶

  1. Split: 70 / 10 / 20 stratified split
  2. Feature engineering: Hour_Sin, Hour_Cos, Is_Night, Log_Amount, Amount_Bin_Small, Amount_Bin_Large (6 new features)
  3. Winsorization: Amount capped at 0.1st and 99.9th percentile (training)
  4. Scaling: RobustScaler on Amount, Time, Log_Amount (fit on training only)
  5. V1–V28: Untouched (PCA-standardized, orthogonal)
  6. No multicollinearity removal: Not needed for PCA components
  7. No encoding: All features are numeric

Recommended Modeling Approach¶

  • Primary metric: PR-AUC (Average Precision) — the 0.172% fraud rate makes accuracy meaningless and ROC-AUC misleadingly optimistic.
  • Class balancing: Use class_weight='balanced' or SMOTE/undersampling.
  • Cross-validation: StratifiedKFold (5 or 10 folds) to preserve class ratios.

Note: Full Chicago-style references are consolidated in the final section of this notebook.

8. Baseline Model: Majority Class Classifier¶

Before training any real model, we establish a floor. A classifier that always predicts "legitimate" will achieve ~99.83% accuracy — proving accuracy is meaningless for this problem.

In [30]:
dummy = DummyClassifier(strategy='most_frequent', random_state=SEED)
dummy.fit(X_train_eng, y_train)
y_pred_dummy = dummy.predict(X_val_eng)

print(classification_report(y_val, y_pred_dummy, target_names=['Legitimate', 'Fraud']))
print(f"Accuracy:  {dummy.score(X_val_eng, y_val):.4f}  ← looks great, means nothing")
print(f"F1 (Fraud): {f1_score(y_val, y_pred_dummy, pos_label=1):.4f}  ← zero")
print(f"PR-AUC:     {average_precision_score(y_val, dummy.predict_proba(X_val_eng)[:,1]):.4f}  ← baseline")

              precision    recall  f1-score   support

  Legitimate       1.00      1.00      1.00     28432
       Fraud       0.00      0.00      0.00        49

    accuracy                           1.00     28481
   macro avg       0.50      0.50      0.50     28481
weighted avg       1.00      1.00      1.00     28481

Accuracy:  0.9983  ← looks great, means nothing
F1 (Fraud): 0.0000  ← zero
PR-AUC:     0.0017  ← baseline

9. Random Forest — Cross-Validation & Hyperparameter Tuning¶

9.1 Why Random Forest?¶

Random Forest is an ensemble of decision trees trained on bootstrap samples with random feature subsets.
For fraud detection it offers several advantages:

  • Handles class imbalance via class_weight='balanced', which reweights each sample inversely
    proportional to its class frequency so the minority fraud class gets ~578× more influence.
  • Non-linear — can capture complex interactions between PCA components without manual feature
    engineering.
  • Robust to outliers — decision splits are rank-based, so extreme values in Amount/V-features
    do not distort the model.
  • Built-in feature importance — out-of-bag impurity-based importance reveals which PCA components
    and engineered features drive fraud detection.

9.2 Hyperparameter Explanation¶

Parameter Value Searched Why
n_estimators 100, 200, 300 More trees → lower variance but diminishing returns; 200–300 is typically the sweet spot for tabular data
max_depth 10, 15, 20, None Controls over-fitting; unlimited depth risks memorising the 492 fraud samples
min_samples_leaf 1, 2, 4 Minimum samples at a leaf; higher values smooth decision boundaries
class_weight 'balanced' (fixed) Corrects the 1:578 imbalance without any resampling; fit on training data only

We use RandomizedSearchCV (not GridSearch) because the parameter space is large.
With n_iter=20 and 5-fold StratifiedKFold, we run 100 fits instead of the full 216 — saving time
while exploring the space probabilistically.
Scoring = average_precision (PR-AUC) is the primary metric because accuracy and ROC-AUC are
misleadingly optimistic at 0.172% fraud rate.

In [ ]:
# 5-fold Stratified CV (preserves 0.172% fraud rate in every fold)
cv_outer = StratifiedKFold(n_splits=5, shuffle=True, random_state=SEED)

scoring = {
    'pr_auc'   : make_scorer(average_precision_score, needs_proba=True),
    'f1'       : make_scorer(f1_score, pos_label=1, zero_division=0),
    'roc_auc'  : 'roc_auc',
    'recall'   : 'recall',
    'precision': 'precision',
}

# Hyperparameter search space 
rf_param_dist = {
    'clf__n_estimators'   : [100, 200, 300],
    'clf__max_depth'      : [10, 15, 20, None],
    'clf__min_samples_leaf': [1, 2, 4],
}

rf_pipeline = Pipeline([
    ('clf', RandomForestClassifier(
        class_weight='balanced',
        random_state=SEED,
        n_jobs=-1
    ))
])

rf_search = RandomizedSearchCV(
    estimator   = rf_pipeline,
    param_distributions = rf_param_dist,
    n_iter      = 20,           # 20 random configs × 5 folds = 100 fits
    scoring     = 'average_precision',
    cv          = cv_outer,
    refit       = True,
    n_jobs      = -1,
    random_state= SEED,
    verbose     = 1,
)

print("Starting Random Forest hyperparameter search (RandomizedSearchCV)...")
t0 = time.time()
rf_search.fit(X_train_eng, y_train)
rf_time = time.time() - t0

print(f"\nSearch completed in {rf_time:.1f}s")
print(f"Best CV PR-AUC : {rf_search.best_score_:.4f}")
print(f"Best params    : {rf_search.best_params_}")

Starting Random Forest hyperparameter search (RandomizedSearchCV)...
Fitting 5 folds for each of 20 candidates, totalling 100 fits

Search completed in 727.1s
Best CV PR-AUC : 0.8529
Best params    : {'clf__n_estimators': 300, 'clf__min_samples_leaf': 1, 'clf__max_depth': None}

9.3 Cross-Validation Results¶

We extract all 20 candidate configurations from the search and plot how CV PR-AUC varies
across the hyperparameter grid — helping us understand sensitivity to each parameter.

In [32]:
rf_cv_df = pd.DataFrame(rf_search.cv_results_)
rf_cv_df = rf_cv_df.sort_values('mean_test_score', ascending=False)

print("Top 10 configurations by CV PR-AUC:")
cols = ['param_clf__n_estimators', 'param_clf__max_depth',
        'param_clf__min_samples_leaf',
        'mean_test_score', 'std_test_score', 'mean_fit_time']
display(rf_cv_df[cols].head(10).round(4))

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(18, 5))
for ax, param, label in zip(
    axes,
    ['param_clf__n_estimators', 'param_clf__max_depth', 'param_clf__min_samples_leaf'],
    ['n_estimators', 'max_depth', 'min_samples_leaf']
):
    tmp = rf_cv_df.copy()
    tmp[param] = tmp[param].astype(str)
    grouped = tmp.groupby(param)['mean_test_score'].agg(['mean', 'std']).reset_index()
    bars = ax.bar(grouped[param], grouped['mean'], yerr=grouped['std'],
                  capsize=5, alpha=0.85, color='steelblue', edgecolor='black')
    ax.set_xlabel(label, fontsize=11)
    ax.set_ylabel('CV PR-AUC', fontsize=11)
    ax.set_title(f'PR-AUC vs {label}', fontsize=11, fontweight='bold')
    ax.set_ylim(0, grouped['mean'].max() * 1.22 + 0.03)
    ax.grid(axis='y', alpha=0.3)
    for bar, val in zip(bars, grouped['mean'].values):
        ax.text(bar.get_x() + bar.get_width() / 2,
                bar.get_height() + 0.007,
                f'{val:.3f}', ha='center', va='bottom', fontsize=9, fontweight='bold')

plt.suptitle('Random Forest — Hyperparameter Sensitivity (CV PR-AUC)',
             fontsize=13, fontweight='bold')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Top 10 configurations by CV PR-AUC:
param_clf__n_estimators param_clf__max_depth param_clf__min_samples_leaf mean_test_score std_test_score mean_fit_time
14 300 None 1 0.8529 0.0505 149.0777
16 200 20 1 0.8502 0.0514 104.2825
5 200 None 2 0.8500 0.0576 98.1856
3 100 None 2 0.8494 0.0551 56.2680
10 100 15 1 0.8473 0.0555 62.9942
6 100 20 2 0.8472 0.0581 63.4349
18 300 15 1 0.8456 0.0541 134.1403
15 100 15 4 0.8453 0.0528 61.3510
11 200 None 4 0.8444 0.0529 102.7695
7 100 15 2 0.8442 0.0592 52.3670

9.4 Full Cross-Validation on Best Configuration¶

We now run full 5-fold CV on the best pipeline to report per-fold metrics with
train/val gap — diagnosing potential overfitting.

In [33]:
best_rf = rf_search.best_estimator_


_scoring_no_pr = {
    'f1'       : make_scorer(f1_score, pos_label=1, zero_division=0),
    'roc_auc'  : 'roc_auc',
    'recall'   : 'recall',
    'precision': 'precision',
}
cv_results_rf = cross_validate(
    best_rf, X_train_eng, y_train,
    cv=cv_outer, scoring=_scoring_no_pr,
    return_train_score=True, n_jobs=-1
)


_pr_train, _pr_val = [], []
for tr_idx, va_idx in cv_outer.split(X_train_eng, y_train):
    Xtr, Xva = X_train_eng.iloc[tr_idx], X_train_eng.iloc[va_idx]
    ytr, yva = y_train.iloc[tr_idx],     y_train.iloc[va_idx]
    best_rf.fit(Xtr, ytr)
    _pr_train.append(average_precision_score(ytr, best_rf.predict_proba(Xtr)[:, 1]))
    _pr_val.append(  average_precision_score(yva, best_rf.predict_proba(Xva)[:, 1]))
cv_results_rf['train_pr_auc'] = np.array(_pr_train)
cv_results_rf['test_pr_auc']  = np.array(_pr_val)

best_rf.fit(X_train_eng, y_train)

print("\nRandom Forest (Best Params) — 5-Fold Stratified CV\n")
print(f"{'Metric':<12} {'Train Mean':>12} {'Train Std':>10} {'Val Mean':>10} {'Val Std':>8} {'Gap':>8}")
print("-" * 62)
for m in ['pr_auc', 'f1', 'roc_auc', 'recall', 'precision']:
    tr = cv_results_rf[f'train_{m}']
    va = cv_results_rf[f'test_{m}']
    print(f"{m:<12} {tr.mean():>12.4f} {tr.std():>10.4f} {va.mean():>10.4f} {va.std():>8.4f} {tr.mean()-va.mean():>8.4f}")

fold_df_rf = pd.DataFrame({
    'Fold'         : np.arange(1, 6),
    'Train PR-AUC' : cv_results_rf['train_pr_auc'].round(4),
    'Val PR-AUC'   : cv_results_rf['test_pr_auc'].round(4),
    'Train F1'     : cv_results_rf['train_f1'].round(4),
    'Val F1'       : cv_results_rf['test_f1'].round(4),
    'Val Recall'   : cv_results_rf['test_recall'].round(4),
    'Val Precision': cv_results_rf['test_precision'].round(4),
})
print(); display(fold_df_rf)

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(14, 5))
x = np.arange(5); w = 0.35
for ax, (metric, title) in zip(axes, [('pr_auc', 'PR-AUC'), ('f1', 'F1-Score')]):
    b1 = ax.bar(x - w/2, cv_results_rf[f'train_{metric}'], w,
                label='Train', color='#2471a3', edgecolor='black', alpha=0.9)
    b2 = ax.bar(x + w/2, cv_results_rf[f'test_{metric}'],  w,
                label='Validation', color='#85c1e9', edgecolor='black', alpha=0.9)
    ax.set_xticks(x); ax.set_xticklabels([f'Fold {i+1}' for i in range(5)])
    ax.set_ylim(0, 1.15); ax.set_ylabel(title, fontsize=11)
    ax.set_title(f'RF — {title} per Fold', fontsize=11, fontweight='bold')
    ax.legend(fontsize=9); ax.grid(axis='y', alpha=0.3)
    for bar in list(b1) + list(b2):
        ax.text(bar.get_x() + bar.get_width() / 2, bar.get_height() + 0.01,
                f'{bar.get_height():.3f}', ha='center', va='bottom', fontsize=8)

plt.suptitle('Random Forest — 5-Fold Cross-Validation', fontsize=13, fontweight='bold')
plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()

Random Forest (Best Params) — 5-Fold Stratified CV

Metric         Train Mean  Train Std   Val Mean  Val Std      Gap
--------------------------------------------------------------
pr_auc             1.0000     0.0000     0.8529   0.0505   0.1471
f1                 1.0000     0.0000     0.8402   0.0502   0.1598
roc_auc            1.0000     0.0000     0.9612   0.0210   0.0388
recall             1.0000     0.0000     0.7536   0.0855   0.2464
precision          1.0000     0.0000     0.9566   0.0094   0.0434
Fold Train PR-AUC Val PR-AUC Train F1 Val F1 Val Recall Val Precision
0 1 1.0000 0.8700 1.0000 0.8067 0.6957 0.9600
1 2 1.0000 0.7578 1.0000 0.7863 0.6667 0.9583
2 3 1.0000 0.8486 1.0000 0.8067 0.6957 0.9600
3 4 1.0000 0.8962 1.0000 0.8906 0.8261 0.9661
4 5 1.0000 0.8921 1.0000 0.9104 0.8841 0.9385

9.5 Validation-Set Evaluation (Best Model)¶

After hyperparameter tuning we evaluate the best model on the held-out validation set,
including threshold optimisation for maximum F1.

In [34]:
best_rf.fit(X_train_eng, y_train)
y_val_proba_rf = best_rf.predict_proba(X_val_eng)[:, 1]

prec_rf, rec_rf, thr_rf = precision_recall_curve(y_val, y_val_proba_rf)
f1_thr_rf    = 2 * prec_rf[:-1] * rec_rf[:-1] / (prec_rf[:-1] + rec_rf[:-1] + 1e-8)
best_idx_rf  = int(np.argmax(f1_thr_rf))
best_thr_rf  = thr_rf[best_idx_rf]
y_val_pred_rf = (y_val_proba_rf >= best_thr_rf).astype(int)

print(f"Optimal threshold : {best_thr_rf:.4f}")
print(f"\n{classification_report(y_val, y_val_pred_rf, target_names=['Legit','Fraud'], digits=4)}")

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 4, figsize=(24, 5))


axes[0].plot(rec_rf[:-1], prec_rf[:-1], lw=2, color='steelblue', label='PR curve')
axes[0].scatter(rec_rf[best_idx_rf], prec_rf[best_idx_rf],
                s=130, c='red', zorder=6, label=f'Best t={best_thr_rf:.3f}')
axes[0].axhline(y_val.mean(), color='gray', ls='--', alpha=0.6,
                label=f'No-skill ({y_val.mean():.3f})')
axes[0].set_xlabel('Recall'); axes[0].set_ylabel('Precision')
axes[0].set_title(f'RF — PR Curve (AP={average_precision_score(y_val, y_val_proba_rf):.4f})',
                  fontweight='bold')
axes[0].legend(fontsize=8); axes[0].grid(True, alpha=0.3)
axes[0].set_xlim(0, 1.02); axes[0].set_ylim(0, 1.05)

fpr_rf, tpr_rf, _ = roc_curve(y_val, y_val_proba_rf)
axes[1].plot(fpr_rf, tpr_rf, lw=2, color='steelblue',
             label=f'ROC-AUC = {roc_auc_score(y_val, y_val_proba_rf):.4f}')
axes[1].plot([0, 1], [0, 1], 'k--', alpha=0.4, label='Random')
axes[1].set_xlabel('False Positive Rate'); axes[1].set_ylabel('True Positive Rate')
axes[1].set_title('RF — ROC Curve', fontweight='bold')
axes[1].legend(fontsize=9); axes[1].grid(True, alpha=0.3)


cm_rf = confusion_matrix(y_val, y_val_pred_rf)
ConfusionMatrixDisplay(cm_rf, display_labels=['Legit', 'Fraud']).plot(
    cmap='Blues', ax=axes[2], values_format='d')
axes[2].set_title(f'RF — Confusion Matrix\n(threshold = {best_thr_rf:.3f})',
                  fontweight='bold')


rf_imp = pd.Series(best_rf.named_steps['clf'].feature_importances_,
                   index=X_train_eng.columns).sort_values(ascending=True).tail(15)
axes[3].barh(rf_imp.index, rf_imp.values, color='steelblue', edgecolor='black', alpha=0.85)
axes[3].set_title('RF — Top 15 Feature Importances\n(mean impurity decrease)',
                  fontweight='bold')
axes[3].set_xlabel('Importance')
axes[3].grid(axis='x', alpha=0.3)

plt.suptitle('Random Forest — Validation Set Evaluation', fontsize=13, fontweight='bold')
plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()

rf_val_pr_auc  = average_precision_score(y_val, y_val_proba_rf)
rf_val_roc_auc = roc_auc_score(y_val, y_val_proba_rf)
print(f"\nVal PR-AUC   : {rf_val_pr_auc:.4f}")
print(f"Val ROC-AUC  : {rf_val_roc_auc:.4f}")
print(f"Val F1       : {f1_score(y_val, y_val_pred_rf):.4f}")
print(f"Val Recall   : {recall_score(y_val, y_val_pred_rf):.4f}")
print(f"Val Precision: {precision_score(y_val, y_val_pred_rf):.4f}")
print(f"Training time: {rf_time:.1f}s")

Optimal threshold : 0.4800

              precision    recall  f1-score   support

       Legit     0.9995    0.9999    0.9997     28432
       Fraud     0.9459    0.7143    0.8140        49

    accuracy                         0.9994     28481
   macro avg     0.9727    0.8571    0.9068     28481
weighted avg     0.9994    0.9994    0.9994     28481


Val PR-AUC   : 0.7850
Val ROC-AUC  : 0.9351
Val F1       : 0.8140
Val Recall   : 0.7143
Val Precision: 0.9459
Training time: 727.1s

10. Logistic Regression — Cross-Validation & Hyperparameter Tuning¶

10.1 Why Logistic Regression?¶

Logistic Regression is the standard interpretable linear baseline for binary classification.
Despite its simplicity, it performs well on PCA-transformed features because:

  • V1–V28 are orthogonal and already centred — exactly the conditions where logistic regression
    thrives (no multicollinearity, no need for non-linear interactions among V-features).
  • It produces calibrated probabilities natively, making threshold optimisation straightforward.
  • Coefficients are directly interpretable: which PCA components and engineered features have the
    strongest linear association with fraud?

10.2 Hyperparameter Explanation¶

Parameter Value Searched Why
C (inverse regularisation) 0.001, 0.01, 0.1, 1, 10, 100 Small C → stronger regularisation → prevents over-fitting on 492 fraud samples; large C → trusts data more
penalty 'l1', 'l2' L2 shrinks all coefficients; L1 performs automatic feature selection by zeroing weak predictors
solver 'liblinear', 'saga' liblinear handles L1+L2 well on medium datasets; saga supports all penalties and scales to large n
class_weight 'balanced' (fixed) Same reasoning as Random Forest — corrects 1:578 imbalance
max_iter 2000 (fixed) Ensures convergence for all C values without iterating unnecessarily

GridSearchCV is used here (instead of Random) because the search space is small
(6 × 2 × 2 = 24 combinations × 5 folds = 120 fits) — exhaustive search is feasible.

In [35]:
lr_param_grid = {
    'clf__C'      : [0.001, 0.01, 0.1, 1, 10, 100],
    'clf__penalty': ['l1', 'l2'],
    'clf__solver' : ['liblinear'],   # supports both L1 and L2
}

lr_pipeline = Pipeline([
    ('clf', LogisticRegression(
        class_weight='balanced',
        max_iter=2000,
        random_state=SEED
    ))
])

lr_search = GridSearchCV(
    estimator = lr_pipeline,
    param_grid = lr_param_grid,
    scoring   = 'average_precision',
    cv        = cv_outer,
    refit     = True,
    n_jobs    = -1,
    verbose   = 1,
)

print("Starting Logistic Regression grid search (GridSearchCV)...")
t0 = time.time()
lr_search.fit(X_train_eng, y_train)
lr_time = time.time() - t0

print(f"\nSearch completed in {lr_time:.1f}s")
print(f"Best CV PR-AUC : {lr_search.best_score_:.4f}")
print(f"Best params    : {lr_search.best_params_}")

Starting Logistic Regression grid search (GridSearchCV)...
Fitting 5 folds for each of 12 candidates, totalling 60 fits

Search completed in 260.9s
Best CV PR-AUC : 0.7800
Best params    : {'clf__C': 0.01, 'clf__penalty': 'l2', 'clf__solver': 'liblinear'}

10.3 Cross-Validation Results — Regularisation Sensitivity¶

In [36]:
lr_cv_df = pd.DataFrame(lr_search.cv_results_)
lr_cv_df['param_clf__C'] = lr_cv_df['param_clf__C'].astype(float)

print("Top 10 configurations by CV PR-AUC:")
cols_lr = ['param_clf__C', 'param_clf__penalty', 'param_clf__solver',
           'mean_test_score', 'std_test_score', 'mean_fit_time']
display(lr_cv_df.sort_values('mean_test_score', ascending=False)[cols_lr].head(10).round(4))

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(14, 5))
for ax, (penalty, color) in zip(axes, [('l1', '#c0392b'), ('l2', '#2980b9')]):
    sub = lr_cv_df[lr_cv_df['param_clf__penalty'] == penalty].sort_values('param_clf__C')
    ax.errorbar(np.log10(sub['param_clf__C']),
                sub['mean_test_score'], yerr=sub['std_test_score'],
                marker='o', lw=2, capsize=5, color=color,
                markerfacecolor='white', markeredgewidth=2, markersize=8)
    ax.set_xlabel('log₁₀(C)   ← stronger reg.   weaker reg. →', fontsize=10)
    ax.set_ylabel('CV PR-AUC', fontsize=11)
    ax.set_title(f'LR ({penalty.upper()} penalty) — C Sensitivity', fontsize=11, fontweight='bold')
    ax.grid(True, alpha=0.3)
    best_row = sub.loc[sub['mean_test_score'].idxmax()]
    ax.axvline(np.log10(best_row['param_clf__C']), color='red', ls='--', alpha=0.7,
               label=f"Best C={best_row['param_clf__C']}  PR-AUC={best_row['mean_test_score']:.3f}")
    ax.legend(fontsize=9)
    for _, row in sub.iterrows():
        ax.annotate(f"{row['mean_test_score']:.3f}",
                    (np.log10(row['param_clf__C']), row['mean_test_score']),
                    textcoords='offset points', xytext=(0, 10), ha='center', fontsize=8)

plt.suptitle('Logistic Regression — Regularisation Sensitivity (CV PR-AUC)',
             fontsize=13, fontweight='bold')
plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()

Top 10 configurations by CV PR-AUC:
param_clf__C param_clf__penalty param_clf__solver mean_test_score std_test_score mean_fit_time
3 0.0100 l2 liblinear 0.7800 0.0401 6.1225
5 0.1000 l2 liblinear 0.7787 0.0396 8.6762
2 0.0100 l1 liblinear 0.7785 0.0403 8.1090
4 0.1000 l1 liblinear 0.7784 0.0394 102.2299
7 1.0000 l2 liblinear 0.7766 0.0391 10.1965
6 1.0000 l1 liblinear 0.7766 0.0389 169.7923
10 100.0000 l1 liblinear 0.7766 0.0388 126.8493
9 10.0000 l2 liblinear 0.7766 0.0388 10.8119
11 100.0000 l2 liblinear 0.7766 0.0388 11.4214
8 10.0000 l1 liblinear 0.7766 0.0388 176.9354

10.4 Full Cross-Validation on Best Configuration¶

In [37]:
best_lr = lr_search.best_estimator_

_scoring_no_pr = {
    'f1'       : make_scorer(f1_score, pos_label=1, zero_division=0),
    'roc_auc'  : 'roc_auc',
    'recall'   : 'recall',
    'precision': 'precision',
}
cv_results_lr = cross_validate(
    best_lr, X_train_eng, y_train,
    cv=cv_outer, scoring=_scoring_no_pr,
    return_train_score=True, n_jobs=-1
)

_pr_train_lr, _pr_val_lr = [], []
for tr_idx, va_idx in cv_outer.split(X_train_eng, y_train):
    Xtr, Xva = X_train_eng.iloc[tr_idx], X_train_eng.iloc[va_idx]
    ytr, yva = y_train.iloc[tr_idx],     y_train.iloc[va_idx]
    best_lr.fit(Xtr, ytr)
    _pr_train_lr.append(average_precision_score(ytr, best_lr.predict_proba(Xtr)[:, 1]))
    _pr_val_lr.append(  average_precision_score(yva, best_lr.predict_proba(Xva)[:, 1]))
cv_results_lr['train_pr_auc'] = np.array(_pr_train_lr)
cv_results_lr['test_pr_auc']  = np.array(_pr_val_lr)
best_lr.fit(X_train_eng, y_train)

print("Logistic Regression (Best Params) — 5-Fold Stratified CV\n")
print(f"{'Metric':<12} {'Train Mean':>12} {'Train Std':>10} {'Val Mean':>10} {'Val Std':>8} {'Gap':>8}")
print("-" * 62)
for m in ['pr_auc', 'f1', 'roc_auc', 'recall', 'precision']:
    tr = cv_results_lr[f'train_{m}']
    va = cv_results_lr[f'test_{m}']
    print(f"{m:<12} {tr.mean():>12.4f} {tr.std():>10.4f} {va.mean():>10.4f} {va.std():>8.4f} {tr.mean()-va.mean():>8.4f}")

fold_df_lr = pd.DataFrame({
    'Fold'         : np.arange(1, 6),
    'Train PR-AUC' : cv_results_lr['train_pr_auc'].round(4),
    'Val PR-AUC'   : cv_results_lr['test_pr_auc'].round(4),
    'Train F1'     : cv_results_lr['train_f1'].round(4),
    'Val F1'       : cv_results_lr['test_f1'].round(4),
    'Val Recall'   : cv_results_lr['test_recall'].round(4),
    'Val Precision': cv_results_lr['test_precision'].round(4),
})
display(fold_df_lr)

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(14, 5))
x = np.arange(5); w = 0.35
for ax, (metric, title) in zip(axes, [('pr_auc', 'PR-AUC'), ('f1', 'F1-Score')]):
    b1 = ax.bar(x - w/2, cv_results_lr[f'train_{metric}'], w,
                label='Train', color='#d35400', edgecolor='black', alpha=0.9)
    b2 = ax.bar(x + w/2, cv_results_lr[f'test_{metric}'],  w,
                label='Validation', color='#f0b27a', edgecolor='black', alpha=0.9)
    ax.set_xticks(x); ax.set_xticklabels([f'Fold {i+1}' for i in range(5)])
    ax.set_ylim(0, 1.15); ax.set_ylabel(title, fontsize=11)
    ax.set_title(f'LR — {title} per Fold', fontsize=11, fontweight='bold')
    ax.legend(fontsize=9); ax.grid(axis='y', alpha=0.3)
    for bar in list(b1) + list(b2):
        ax.text(bar.get_x() + bar.get_width() / 2, bar.get_height() + 0.01,
                f'{bar.get_height():.3f}', ha='center', va='bottom', fontsize=8)

plt.suptitle('Logistic Regression — 5-Fold Cross-Validation', fontsize=13, fontweight='bold')
plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()

Logistic Regression (Best Params) — 5-Fold Stratified CV

Metric         Train Mean  Train Std   Val Mean  Val Std      Gap
--------------------------------------------------------------
pr_auc             0.7664     0.0140     0.7668   0.0439  -0.0004
f1                 0.1249     0.0144     0.1215   0.0078   0.0034
roc_auc            0.9933     0.0012     0.9855   0.0087   0.0078
recall             0.9362     0.0104     0.9130   0.0389   0.0232
precision          0.0670     0.0083     0.0651   0.0046   0.0018
Fold Train PR-AUC Val PR-AUC Train F1 Val F1 Val Recall Val Precision
0 1 0.7650 0.7604 0.1190 0.1260 0.9275 0.0676
1 2 0.7907 0.7005 0.1524 0.1302 0.8406 0.0706
2 3 0.7511 0.8280 0.1251 0.1266 0.9130 0.0680
3 4 0.7706 0.7461 0.1156 0.1152 0.9565 0.0613
4 5 0.7545 0.7989 0.1125 0.1096 0.9275 0.0582

10.5 Validation-Set Evaluation (Best Model)¶

In [38]:
best_lr.fit(X_train_eng, y_train)
y_val_proba_lr = best_lr.predict_proba(X_val_eng)[:, 1]

prec_lr, rec_lr, thr_lr = precision_recall_curve(y_val, y_val_proba_lr)
f1_thr_lr    = 2 * prec_lr[:-1] * rec_lr[:-1] / (prec_lr[:-1] + rec_lr[:-1] + 1e-8)
best_idx_lr  = int(np.argmax(f1_thr_lr))
best_thr_lr  = thr_lr[best_idx_lr]
y_val_pred_lr = (y_val_proba_lr >= best_thr_lr).astype(int)

print(f"Optimal threshold : {best_thr_lr:.4f}")
print(f"\n{classification_report(y_val, y_val_pred_lr, target_names=['Legit','Fraud'], digits=4)}")

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 4, figsize=(24, 5))

# 1. PR 
axes[0].plot(rec_lr[:-1], prec_lr[:-1], lw=2, color='darkorange', label='PR curve')
axes[0].scatter(rec_lr[best_idx_lr], prec_lr[best_idx_lr],
                s=130, c='red', zorder=6, label=f'Best t={best_thr_lr:.3f}')
axes[0].axhline(y_val.mean(), color='gray', ls='--', alpha=0.6,
                label=f'No-skill ({y_val.mean():.3f})')
axes[0].set_xlabel('Recall'); axes[0].set_ylabel('Precision')
axes[0].set_title(f'LR — PR Curve (AP={average_precision_score(y_val, y_val_proba_lr):.4f})',
                  fontweight='bold')
axes[0].legend(fontsize=8); axes[0].grid(True, alpha=0.3)
axes[0].set_xlim(0, 1.02); axes[0].set_ylim(0, 1.05)

# 2. ROC
fpr_lr, tpr_lr, _ = roc_curve(y_val, y_val_proba_lr)
axes[1].plot(fpr_lr, tpr_lr, lw=2, color='darkorange',
             label=f'ROC-AUC = {roc_auc_score(y_val, y_val_proba_lr):.4f}')
axes[1].plot([0, 1], [0, 1], 'k--', alpha=0.4, label='Random')
axes[1].set_xlabel('False Positive Rate'); axes[1].set_ylabel('True Positive Rate')
axes[1].set_title('LR — ROC Curve', fontweight='bold')
axes[1].legend(fontsize=9); axes[1].grid(True, alpha=0.3)


cm_lr = confusion_matrix(y_val, y_val_pred_lr)
ConfusionMatrixDisplay(cm_lr, display_labels=['Legit', 'Fraud']).plot(
    cmap='Oranges', ax=axes[2], values_format='d')
axes[2].set_title(f'LR — Confusion Matrix\n(threshold = {best_thr_lr:.3f})', fontweight='bold')


lr_coef = pd.Series(
    best_lr.named_steps['clf'].coef_[0],
    index=X_train_eng.columns
).sort_values(key=abs, ascending=True).tail(15)
coef_colors = ['#e74c3c' if v > 0 else '#3498db' for v in lr_coef.values]
axes[3].barh(lr_coef.index, lr_coef.values, color=coef_colors, edgecolor='black', alpha=0.85)
axes[3].axvline(0, color='black', lw=0.8)
axes[3].set_title('LR — Top 15 Coefficients\n(red = fraud signal, blue = legit signal)',
                  fontweight='bold')
axes[3].set_xlabel('Coefficient value')
axes[3].grid(axis='x', alpha=0.3)

plt.suptitle('Logistic Regression — Validation Set Evaluation', fontsize=13, fontweight='bold')
plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()

lr_val_pr_auc  = average_precision_score(y_val, y_val_proba_lr)
lr_val_roc_auc = roc_auc_score(y_val, y_val_proba_lr)
print(f"\nVal PR-AUC   : {lr_val_pr_auc:.4f}")
print(f"Val ROC-AUC  : {lr_val_roc_auc:.4f}")
print(f"Val F1       : {f1_score(y_val, y_val_pred_lr):.4f}")
print(f"Val Recall   : {recall_score(y_val, y_val_pred_lr):.4f}")
print(f"Val Precision: {precision_score(y_val, y_val_pred_lr):.4f}")
print(f"Training time: {lr_time:.1f}s")

Optimal threshold : 0.9998

              precision    recall  f1-score   support

       Legit     0.9995    0.9997    0.9996     28432
       Fraud     0.8182    0.7347    0.7742        49

    accuracy                         0.9993     28481
   macro avg     0.9089    0.8672    0.8869     28481
weighted avg     0.9992    0.9993    0.9992     28481


Val PR-AUC   : 0.6645
Val ROC-AUC  : 0.9661
Val F1       : 0.7742
Val Recall   : 0.7347
Val Precision: 0.8182
Training time: 260.9s

11. XGBoost — Cross-Validation & Hyperparameter Tuning¶

11.1 Why XGBoost?¶

XGBoost (Extreme Gradient Boosting) builds an ensemble of trees sequentially, each tree
correcting the residual errors of the previous one. For fraud detection:

  • Native imbalance handling via scale_pos_weight — equivalent to class_weight='balanced'
    but integrated directly into the gradient computation.
  • Second-order gradients (Newton's method) — converges faster and more accurately than
    first-order methods (Random Forest uses entropy/gini).
  • Regularisation (reg_alpha L1, reg_lambda L2) embedded in tree building — prevents
    overfitting even with many estimators.
  • State-of-the-art performance on structured tabular data across hundreds of Kaggle competitions.
  • eval_metric='aucpr' directly optimises PR-AUC during training.

11.2 Hyperparameter Explanation¶

Parameter Value Searched Why
n_estimators 200, 400, 600 More rounds → better fit; early stopping prevents overfitting
max_depth 4, 6, 8 Shallow trees (4–6) generalise better; depth 8 may overfit on 492 fraud samples
learning_rate 0.01, 0.05, 0.1 Lower lr → needs more trees but generalises better; 0.05 is the standard starting point
subsample 0.7, 0.9 Row subsampling per tree — reduces variance (like bootstrap in RF)
colsample_bytree 0.7, 0.9 Column subsampling — adds diversity across trees
scale_pos_weight Computed (≈578) Tells XGBoost each fraud sample counts as ~578 legitimate ones

RandomizedSearchCV with n_iter=20 keeps training time manageable (3×3×3×2×2 = 108 full grid).

In [39]:
scale_pos = float((y_train == 0).sum()) / float((y_train == 1).sum())
print(f"scale_pos_weight = {scale_pos:.2f}  (# legitimate / # fraud in training set)")

xgb_param_dist = {
    'n_estimators'   : [200, 400, 600],
    'max_depth'      : [4, 6, 8],
    'learning_rate'  : [0.01, 0.05, 0.1],
    'subsample'      : [0.7, 0.9],
    'colsample_bytree': [0.7, 0.9],
}

xgb_base = xgb.XGBClassifier(
    scale_pos_weight = scale_pos,
    eval_metric      = 'aucpr',
    use_label_encoder= False,
    random_state     = SEED,
    n_jobs           = -1,
    verbosity        = 0,
)

xgb_search = RandomizedSearchCV(
    estimator   = xgb_base,
    param_distributions = xgb_param_dist,
    n_iter      = 20,
    scoring     = 'average_precision',
    cv          = cv_outer,
    refit       = True,
    n_jobs      = -1,
    random_state= SEED,
    verbose     = 1,
)

print("\nStarting XGBoost hyperparameter search...")
t0 = time.time()
xgb_search.fit(X_train_eng, y_train)
xgb_time = time.time() - t0

print(f"\nSearch completed in {xgb_time:.1f}s")
print(f"Best CV PR-AUC : {xgb_search.best_score_:.4f}")
print(f"Best params    : {xgb_search.best_params_}")

scale_pos_weight = 576.87  (# legitimate / # fraud in training set)

Starting XGBoost hyperparameter search...
Fitting 5 folds for each of 20 candidates, totalling 100 fits

Search completed in 83.1s
Best CV PR-AUC : 0.8625
Best params    : {'subsample': 0.9, 'n_estimators': 600, 'max_depth': 6, 'learning_rate': 0.05, 'colsample_bytree': 0.9}

11.3 Cross-Validation Results — Sensitivity to Key Parameters¶

In [40]:
xgb_cv_df = pd.DataFrame(xgb_search.cv_results_)
print("Top 10 configurations by CV PR-AUC:")
cols_xgb = ['param_n_estimators', 'param_max_depth', 'param_learning_rate',
            'param_subsample', 'param_colsample_bytree',
            'mean_test_score', 'std_test_score', 'mean_fit_time']
display(xgb_cv_df.sort_values('mean_test_score', ascending=False)[cols_xgb].head(10).round(4))

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(18, 5))
for ax, (param, label) in zip(axes, [
    ('param_learning_rate', 'learning_rate'),
    ('param_max_depth',     'max_depth'),
    ('param_n_estimators',  'n_estimators'),
]):
    tmp = xgb_cv_df.copy()
    tmp[param] = tmp[param].astype(str)
    grouped = tmp.groupby(param)['mean_test_score'].agg(['mean', 'std']).reset_index()
    bars = ax.bar(grouped[param], grouped['mean'], yerr=grouped['std'],
                  capsize=5, alpha=0.85, color='#e74c3c', edgecolor='black')
    ax.set_xlabel(label, fontsize=11)
    ax.set_ylabel('CV PR-AUC', fontsize=11)
    ax.set_title(f'XGB — {label} vs PR-AUC', fontsize=11, fontweight='bold')
    ax.set_ylim(0, grouped['mean'].max() * 1.22 + 0.03)
    ax.grid(axis='y', alpha=0.3)
    for bar, val in zip(bars, grouped['mean'].values):
        ax.text(bar.get_x() + bar.get_width() / 2,
                bar.get_height() + 0.007,
                f'{val:.3f}', ha='center', va='bottom', fontsize=9, fontweight='bold')

plt.suptitle('XGBoost — Hyperparameter Sensitivity (CV PR-AUC)',
             fontsize=13, fontweight='bold')
plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()

Top 10 configurations by CV PR-AUC:
param_n_estimators param_max_depth param_learning_rate param_subsample param_colsample_bytree mean_test_score std_test_score mean_fit_time
3 600 6 0.0500 0.9000 0.9000 0.8625 0.0538 19.1007
10 400 6 0.0500 0.9000 0.9000 0.8608 0.0538 12.5495
7 400 6 0.1000 0.9000 0.7000 0.8608 0.0553 11.4908
18 600 8 0.1000 0.7000 0.9000 0.8594 0.0534 14.7373
0 600 4 0.0500 0.9000 0.9000 0.8592 0.0565 13.0175
8 600 4 0.1000 0.9000 0.9000 0.8588 0.0572 15.0008
17 600 4 0.0500 0.7000 0.9000 0.8577 0.0577 15.1098
13 400 6 0.0500 0.7000 0.9000 0.8572 0.0542 11.6305

11.4 Full Cross-Validation on Best Configuration¶

In [41]:
best_xgb = xgb_search.best_estimator_

_scoring_no_pr = {
    'f1'       : make_scorer(f1_score, pos_label=1, zero_division=0),
    'roc_auc'  : 'roc_auc',
    'recall'   : 'recall',
    'precision': 'precision',
}
cv_results_xgb = cross_validate(
    best_xgb, X_train_eng, y_train,
    cv=cv_outer, scoring=_scoring_no_pr,
    return_train_score=True, n_jobs=-1
)

_pr_train_xgb, _pr_val_xgb = [], []
for tr_idx, va_idx in cv_outer.split(X_train_eng, y_train):
    Xtr, Xva = X_train_eng.iloc[tr_idx], X_train_eng.iloc[va_idx]
    ytr, yva = y_train.iloc[tr_idx],     y_train.iloc[va_idx]
    best_xgb.fit(Xtr, ytr)
    _pr_train_xgb.append(average_precision_score(ytr, best_xgb.predict_proba(Xtr)[:, 1]))
    _pr_val_xgb.append(  average_precision_score(yva, best_xgb.predict_proba(Xva)[:, 1]))
cv_results_xgb['train_pr_auc'] = np.array(_pr_train_xgb)
cv_results_xgb['test_pr_auc']  = np.array(_pr_val_xgb)
best_xgb.fit(X_train_eng, y_train)

print("XGBoost (Best Params) — 5-Fold Stratified CV\n")
print(f"{'Metric':<12} {'Train Mean':>12} {'Train Std':>10} {'Val Mean':>10} {'Val Std':>8} {'Gap':>8}")
print("-" * 62)
for m in ['pr_auc', 'f1', 'roc_auc', 'recall', 'precision']:
    tr = cv_results_xgb[f'train_{m}']
    va = cv_results_xgb[f'test_{m}']
    print(f"{m:<12} {tr.mean():>12.4f} {tr.std():>10.4f} {va.mean():>10.4f} {va.std():>8.4f} {tr.mean()-va.mean():>8.4f}")

fold_df_xgb = pd.DataFrame({
    'Fold'         : np.arange(1, 6),
    'Train PR-AUC' : cv_results_xgb['train_pr_auc'].round(4),
    'Val PR-AUC'   : cv_results_xgb['test_pr_auc'].round(4),
    'Train F1'     : cv_results_xgb['train_f1'].round(4),
    'Val F1'       : cv_results_xgb['test_f1'].round(4),
    'Val Recall'   : cv_results_xgb['test_recall'].round(4),
    'Val Precision': cv_results_xgb['test_precision'].round(4),
})
display(fold_df_xgb)

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(14, 5))
x = np.arange(5); w = 0.35
for ax, (metric, title) in zip(axes, [('pr_auc', 'PR-AUC'), ('f1', 'F1-Score')]):
    b1 = ax.bar(x - w/2, cv_results_xgb[f'train_{metric}'], w,
                label='Train', color='#c0392b', edgecolor='black', alpha=0.9)
    b2 = ax.bar(x + w/2, cv_results_xgb[f'test_{metric}'],  w,
                label='Validation', color='#f1948a', edgecolor='black', alpha=0.9)
    ax.set_xticks(x); ax.set_xticklabels([f'Fold {i+1}' for i in range(5)])
    ax.set_ylim(0, 1.15); ax.set_ylabel(title, fontsize=11)
    ax.set_title(f'XGB — {title} per Fold', fontsize=11, fontweight='bold')
    ax.legend(fontsize=9); ax.grid(axis='y', alpha=0.3)
    for bar in list(b1) + list(b2):
        ax.text(bar.get_x() + bar.get_width() / 2, bar.get_height() + 0.01,
                f'{bar.get_height():.3f}', ha='center', va='bottom', fontsize=8)

plt.suptitle('XGBoost — 5-Fold Cross-Validation', fontsize=13, fontweight='bold')
plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()
